era_context: The mid-15th century is a period of significant transition in Europe and Asia. The Hundred Years' War between England and France has recently ended (1453), reshaping Western European politics. The Ottoman Empire is consolidating power after the conquest of Constantinople. The Ming Dynasty in China is stable under centralised rule. The Italian Renaissance is flourishing. In Africa, Portuguese explorers are establishing trading posts along the coast.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Indigenous Americas (no written records in European tradition)

events.title: Ivan III (Ivan the Great) consolidates power in Muscovy
events.description: Ivan III, known as Ivan the Great, was consolidating power in Muscovy in 1462. He had recently ascended to the throne and was beginning the process of centralising Muscovite authority and expanding Muscovite territory. In 1462, Ivan was establishing himself as a major power in Eastern Europe and beginning the process of throwing off Mongol overlordship. His reign would mark the beginning of Muscovy's rise as a major European power.
key_figures: Ivan III (Ivan the Great, Grand Prince of Muscovy)

sources.name: Russian chronicles (Povest' vremennykh let and later compilations)

events.certainty_note: Ivan III's accession to power in 1462 is well-documented in Russian chronicles and Muscovite records.
cross_references: Part of Muscovite rise to power (1462–1505)

events.title: Venetian-Ottoman War (First Venetian-Ottoman War) begins
events.description: The First Venetian-Ottoman War (1463–1479) was beginning in 1462 or early 1463. Venice, a major Mediterranean naval power, was entering into conflict with the expanding Ottoman Empire over control of trade routes and territories in the Eastern Mediterranean. The war would be costly for both sides and would ultimately result in Ottoman victory and Venetian territorial losses. The conflict was driven by Ottoman expansion and Venetian efforts to maintain its commercial dominance.
key_figures: Venetian Republic (represented by various doges and commanders)
events.certainty_note: The First Venetian-Ottoman War is dated to 1463–1479 in most sources, though tensions and preliminary conflicts may have occurred in 1462.

events.title: Thirteen Years' War between Poland-Lithuania and Teutonic Order continues
events.description: The Thirteen Years' War (1454–1466) between the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth and the Teutonic Order was ongoing in 1462. The conflict had been initiated by the Prussian Confederation's appeal to Poland against Teutonic rule. In 1462, both sides were engaged in military campaigns and diplomatic negotiations. The war would continue for four more years before concluding with the Peace of Thorn in 1466.
key_figures: Ludwig von Vietinghoff (Grand Master of the Teutonic Order)
events.certainty_note: The Thirteen Years' War is well-documented; 1462 falls within the confirmed period of conflict.

events.title: Zhengtong Emperor's reign in Ming China; political consolidation
events.description: The Zhengtong Emperor (Zhu Qizhen) was ruling the Ming Dynasty in 1462. He had been restored to the throne in 1457 after a period of captivity and deposition. His reign was marked by administrative consolidation and the continuation of Ming centralised rule. In 1462, the Ming Dynasty was stable and prosperous, with the emperor consolidating his authority. However, his reign would be marked by the growing influence of eunuchs in court politics.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Zhengtong Emperor's reign is well-documented in Ming court records.
cross_references: Part of Ming Dynasty political history (1368–1644)

events.title: Mehmed II consolidates Ottoman power; campaigns continue
events.description: Mehmed II, the Conqueror, continued his consolidation of Ottoman power in 1462. Following the conquest of Constantinople in 1453, Mehmed was systematically subjugating rival powers in Anatolia and the Balkans. In 1462, he was engaged in military campaigns to expand Ottoman territory and eliminate threats to Ottoman hegemony. His campaigns were part of a broader strategy to establish Ottoman dominance in the Eastern Mediterranean and Balkans.
events.certainty_note: Mehmed II's military activities are well-documented in Ottoman and Venetian sources.

events.title: Italian Renaissance: Piero della Francesca and Sandro Botticelli active
events.description: In 1462, major figures of the Italian Renaissance were active and producing significant works. Piero della Francesca was completing major commissions in Urbino and Arezzo, while Sandro Botticelli was establishing himself as a major painter in Florence. The year 1462 was a period of intense artistic activity and innovation in Italy, with the Renaissance in full flourish. These artists were developing new techniques in perspective, light, and form that would define Renaissance art.
key_figures: Federico da Montefeltro (Duke of Urbino, patron)

sources.name: Giorgio Vasari, Lives of the Artists

events.certainty_note: The careers of Piero della Francesca and Sandro Botticelli are well-documented, though specific works and dates can sometimes vary.
cross_references: Part of Italian Renaissance cultural flourishing (14th–16th centuries)

events.title: Portuguese exploration: continued expansion along African coast
events.description: Portuguese explorers, under the patronage of Prince Henry the Navigator, continued their systematic exploration of the African coast in 1462. Portuguese trading posts were being established along the Guinea coast, and explorers were pushing further south. These expeditions were laying the groundwork for the later Age of Exploration and establishing Portuguese commercial dominance in West African trade.

sources.name: Portuguese royal archives and navigation records

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration of this period is documented, though specific dates for individual voyages can vary.
cross_references: Part of Portuguese Age of Exploration (1415–1500s)

disconfirming_evidence: The characterisation of Ivan III's accession in 1462 as the beginning of Muscovy's rise to power is somewhat simplified. Modern scholarship emphasises that Ivan III's consolidation of power was a gradual process that took several decades, and that his early reign was marked by significant challenges and setbacks. Additionally, the claim that he was 'throwing off Mongol overlordship' in 1462 is anachronistic; the formal end of Mongol overlordship is typically dated to 1480, when Ivan III refused to pay tribute to the Golden Horde. The dating of the First Venetian-Ottoman War to 1462 is also uncertain; most sources date it to 1463–1479, with tensions and preliminary conflicts possibly occurring in 1462.
historiographic_note: The year 1462 is well-documented in European sources, particularly for military and political events. Italian Renaissance sources are particularly rich. Ming Dynasty records are extensive and contemporary. Russian chronicles are available but often compiled much later than the events they describe. However, as with other years in this period, non-European regions are underrepresented. The sources available are often written from the perspective of European powers or East Asian courts. The historical record for 1462 is heavily weighted toward European and East Asian events, with limited documentation of events in Africa, the Americas, and Central Asia.

graph_edges.note: Ivan III's accession in 1462 began his long reign of consolidation and expansion
Tensions in 1462 led to the formal outbreak of the First Venetian-Ottoman War in 1463
The Thirteen Years' War continued and concluded in 1466
